verbGlossary.ACTIVATES: {source} activity triggers a dormant risk or sets {target} mechanism into an active state (e.g., demolition activates environmental risk when hazardous materials are disturbed)
verbGlossary.APPORTIONS: {source} activity distributes {target} among parties through contractual or commercial means (e.g., bidding apportions risk allocation across subcontractors via scope assignments)
verbGlossary.CATCHES: {source} activity intercepts and identifies {target} before it propagates to field construction (e.g., submittal review catches design risk by flagging conflicts before fabrication)
verbGlossary.CLOSES: {source} activity formally resolves or retires {target} item from the active register (e.g., final inspections close regulatory and permit risk by obtaining certificate of occupancy)
verbGlossary.COMPRESSES: {source} activity reduces available schedule slack, intensifying time-related {target} (e.g., above-ceiling coordination compresses schedule risk when multiple trades stack into the same window)
verbGlossary.CONCENTRATES: {source} activity brings multiple {target}s into the same physical or temporal zone, amplifying their combined effect (e.g., above-ceiling coordination concentrates coordination risk among MEP trades)
verbGlossary.CONSTRAINS: {source} activity creates a hard dependency that limits flexibility and locks in downstream {target} (e.g., roofing constrains schedule risk because interior finishes cannot proceed until the building is watertight)
verbGlossary.CONSUMES: {source} activity uses up schedule or budget buffer that was reserved against {target} (e.g., material delivery delays consume schedule buffer)
verbGlossary.COVERS: {source} activity provides ongoing protection against {target} through a defined period or mechanism (e.g., warranty period covers residual risk of latent defects)
verbGlossary.CREATES: {source} activity generates a new {target} that did not previously exist on the project (e.g., design development creates coordination risk when multiple disciplines produce overlapping drawings)
verbGlossary.DEFINES: {source} activity specifies the content or parameters of {target} management element (e.g., preconstruction planning defines mitigation actions for each identified risk)
verbGlossary.DETECTS: {source} activity uncovers an existing {target} condition that was previously hidden (e.g., testing adjusting and balancing detects residual risk in mechanical systems that were not performing to spec)
verbGlossary.DRIVES: {source} activity is the primary cause behind {target} metric increasing over the project lifecycle (e.g., design development drives change order rate through scope revisions and RFI-generated changes)
verbGlossary.ENCOUNTERS: {source} activity runs directly into {target} condition in the field (e.g., earthwork encounters geotechnical risk when soil conditions differ from borings)
verbGlossary.ESTABLISHES: {source} activity creates the initial baseline or structure for a risk management element (e.g., programming establishes {target} register for the project)
verbGlossary.EXPOSES TO: {source} activity creates vulnerability to a specific {target} by the nature of the work involved (e.g., steel erection exposes the project to construction execution risk from crane operations and high-elevation work)
verbGlossary.FINALIZES: {source} activity locks in the final state of {target} element, closing out adjustments (e.g., contract closeout finalizes risk allocation by settling all remaining claims and retention)
verbGlossary.HEIGHTENS: {source} activity increases the severity or probability of an already-{target} (e.g., structural steel erection heightens safety risk due to elevated work and heavy lifts)
verbGlossary.IDENTIFIES: {source} activity discovers or catalogs {target} for the first time (e.g., programming identifies geotechnical risk from initial site assessment and soil reports)
verbGlossary.INTRODUCES: {source} activity brings a new {target} into the project that must be managed going forward (e.g., fabrication introduces schedule risk through long-lead item dependencies)
verbGlossary.MATERIALIZES: {source} activity is the point where a previously theoretical {target} becomes a real-world condition (e.g., material delivery materializes supply chain risk when shipments arrive late or damaged)
verbGlossary.MITIGATES: {source} activity directly reduces the probability or impact of {target} (e.g., safety orientation mitigates safety risk by training workers on site-specific hazards)
verbGlossary.POPULATES: {source} activity fills a risk assessment framework with project-specific data (e.g., preconstruction planning populates {target} matrix with likelihood and impact scores)
verbGlossary.PRICES: {source} activity assigns a monetary value to {target} through competitive or estimating processes (e.g., bidding prices labor risk as subcontractors factor labor availability into their bids)
verbGlossary.QUANTIFIES: {source} activity determines the dollar magnitude of {target} or exposure (e.g., estimating quantifies financial risk by calculating budget against known cost uncertainties)
verbGlossary.RECONCILES: {source} activity compares actual {target} outcomes against planned reserves and settles the difference (e.g., contract closeout reconciles contingency burn rate against actual risk events)
verbGlossary.REDUCES: {source} activity lowers a measurable {target} through proactive intervention (e.g., safety orientation reduces incident rate through hazard awareness training)
verbGlossary.RESOLVES: {source} activity eliminates or settles {target} by completing the required actions (e.g., permitting resolves regulatory risk by obtaining required approvals)
verbGlossary.REVEALS: {source} activity makes a hidden {target} visible through testing, inspection, or operational activity (e.g., integrated systems testing reveals coordination risk when systems fail to interoperate)
verbGlossary.SCREENS FOR: {source} activity evaluates the project for the presence of {target} category without yet quantifying it (e.g., programming screens for environmental risk through site history and contamination assessments)
verbGlossary.SIZES: {source} activity determines the magnitude of {target} reserve or buffer (e.g., estimating sizes contingency reserve based on project risk profile and historical overrun rates)
verbGlossary.SURFACES: {source} activity brings a latent defect or {target} condition to attention through systematic review (e.g., punch list surfaces residual risk by documenting incomplete or deficient work items)
verbGlossary.TESTS: {source} activity validates whether {target} condition exists or whether a mitigation is working (e.g., systems startup tests construction execution risk by running equipment under operating conditions)
verbGlossary.UPDATES: {source} activity provides new information that changes the assessment of {target} (e.g., performance verification updates risk score based on actual system performance data)
verbGlossary.VALIDATES: {source} activity confirms that {target} mitigation or response has been successfully implemented (e.g., final inspections validate risk mitigation completion rate by verifying all corrective actions are closed)
